data.title: FDIS: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For February
data.description:
This article reports industry metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector. It is also relevant to analyze sector ETFs like Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) and Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FDIS), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by subsector (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

Both value and quality scores have significantly deteriorated in the auto and components industry.

Momentum

The next chart plots momentum scores based on median returns.

Interpretation

According to my monthly S&P 500 dashboard, the consumer discretionary sector as a whole is overvalued by about 11% based on 11-year averages. Nevertheless, consumer services are still attractive, with an excellent quality score and marginal undervaluation relative to the historical baseline. The durables/apparel and auto/components subsectors are slightly overvalued, which may be justified for the former by a good quality score. The retailing subsector has the lowest value score, while auto and components have the lowest quality score.

FDIS fast facts

Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (FDIS) was launched on 10/21/2013 and tracks the MSCI USA IMI Consumer Discretionary Index. FDIS has a total expense ratio of 0.08%, on par with XLY. FDIS holds 268 stocks including large, mid- and small caps, whereas XLY only has 52 constituents in large companies. The portfolio is very concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 56% of asset value, and the top two names, Amazon and Tesla, weigh 23% an 16.9%.

In summary, FDIS is a good choice for long-term investors seeking capital-weighted exposure in consumer cyclicals. However, higher liquidity makes XLY a better trading instrument. Investors who are concerned by the weight of Amazon and Tesla may prefer the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Discretionary ETF (RSPD).

Dashboard List

I use the first table to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells us that a retail company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0461 (or price/earnings below 21.69) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. The stocks below are part of the list sent a few weeks ago, based on data available at this time.

It is a rotational model with a statistical bias toward excess returns on the long-term, not the result of an analysis of each stock.

data.title: Doximity: Rating Downgrade As Near-Term Upside Is Priced In
data.description:
Investment Action

I recommended a buy rating for Doximity (NYSE:DOCS) when I wrote about it on 11th January this year, as I saw the share price pull back as a solid entry point, given that DOCS was seeing strong engagement metrics. Over the past few weeks, DOCS's share price has soared past my price target to ~$77 today, and since the earnings are out, I thought to give an update on my view. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I downgraded my rating from buy to hold. I believe the expected growth slowdown will not sit well with the market, and when DOCS does print a softer growth in FY 2026, the share price will likely take a beating. As much as I like the long-term growth outlook, I would recommend readers lock in any profits they have today and wait until DOCS shows signs of growth accelerating back to 2-3x market level.

Review

DOCS reported a strong Q3 2025 revenue performance of $168.6 million, up 25% y/y. This performance was driven by strong demand from pharma clients, particularly within its top 20 customer cohort, which maintained a net revenue retention rate [NRR] of 122%. The overall customer base also showed strength, with overall NRR at 117%, reflecting increasing adoption of modules across existing clients. Gross margin remained robust at 93.3%, up 50 bps y/y. Combined together, adj EBITDA saw a solid y/y growth of 39% to $102 million, with EBITDA margins expanding to 60.5%, the highest in the DOCS operating history.

There are several key drivers that should continue to support DOCS growth ahead.

Firstly, its market share gains. According to management, the digital healthcare ad market is expected to grow 5-7% annually, and assuming this has been the trend over the past few years, DOCS has shown that it is able to capture share (growth over the past few years has been ~2-3x the market rate). In the Q3 2025 quarter, DOCS also demonstrated its ability to secure larger deals, with the number of >$10 million brand clients increasing to four this quarter, including its first-ever >$15 million advertiser. The fact that DOCS sees positive traction here shows that DOCS's value proposition is increasingly being recognized by large pharma companies (which tend to be more pragmatic).

Secondly, the value proposition of DOCS should continue to improve with more adoption of its client portal. As a recap, the DOCS client portal allows advertisers to manage campaigns and analyze performance. As of Q3 2025, more than 50% of pharma advertisers are now using the portal, and it was noted that those who have adopted it are increasing their ad budgets at a faster rate than those who haven’t. Apparently, clients are seeing great value in using this portal. As I have shared previously, the more users that DOCS brings into this portal, the more it will expand more endpoints for advertisers to target, and also, with all the data points collected, it is able to provide more insights (perhaps a more accurate return-on-marketing-dollar estimate). Hence, it was very positive to hear that DOCS has expanded its reach by onboarding 10 agency partners to the portal, helping bring in smaller pharma clients who may not have previously engaged with DOCS.

Thirdly, DOCS's new ad products have proven to be solid growth drivers, growing over 100% y/y in Q3 2025. These products now contribute >20% of pharma-related revenue. The rate of growth indicates that underlying demand is very strong and also shows that advertisers are seeking to engage physicians outside of the standard newsfeed channel. Suppose this growth rate sustains; new ad products could very quickly become a sizable portion of DOCS's total revenue.

Our modules that sit outside of the newsfeed, point-of-care and formulary, grew by more than 100% year-over-year combined. We also sold a large number of programs on a multi-module integrated basis, which contributed to much larger deal sizes. Q3 2025 call

Valuation

DOCS was cheap when I wrote in January, but I don’t think it is still cheap at $77. DOCS trades at 40.5x forward EBITDA at the current share price, which I think is too high. Granted that the business has a good growth runway ahead, the thing is, growth is expected to decelerate significantly in Q4 2025 and FY 2026. According to the management, Q4 2025 revenue guidance implies just 13% y/y growth, which is a significant drop from the 25% growth seen in Q3 2025. Management also indicated that it expects growth to revert to only ~2x the market rate (around 11-12%) in FY 2026, which is a sizable step-down from the growth seen on a year-to-date basis. I am not a fan of this setup as it creates an “air pocket” over the next 12 months that could cause investors to sell off their holdings when the headline growth figures are poor (yes, even though management has already guided for this, when the headline shows poor growth in FY 2026, I believe the market will not take it positively). Moreover, DOC stock has already surged ~30+% since the Q3 2025 earnings; there is a lot of profit on the table for investors to take and sit out until growth accelerates.

Final thoughts

My recommendation is to take profits at current levels and wait for a better entry point. While I still think DOCS is a high-quality business with strong margins and long-term growth potential, the stock has already priced in much of the near-term upside. The expected growth deceleration in FY 2026 presents a real risk, as I expect investors to react negatively when actual results confirm a slowdown, even if it has been guided for in advance. As such, at 40.5x forward EBITDA, I think the stock is no longer cheap.

data.title: Smith & Wesson Brands: High-Yield Concern Going Through An Inflection Year
data.description:
Today, we put Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. (NASDAQ:SWBI) in the spotlight for the first time since my last article on this well-known small-cap manufacturer almost exactly one year ago. After a tough year in what was an inflection year in 2024, the shares have started to move up here early in 2025. The stock also yields nearly five percent. Which way will the equity head over the coming quarters? An updated analysis of SWBI follows below.

Smith & Wesson Brands, Inc. has recently completely transitioned to its new headquarters in Tennessee from its long-term home of Springfield, MA. The migration was helpful both on a regulatory and cost front. Management estimated at the time that the move would add 10 to 12 cents a share in EPS annually. The company manufactures, markets and distributes both handgun and rifles and associated products under the Smith & Wesson, M&P, and Gemtech brands. Among many well-known firearms, the company manufactures the .357 Magnum of Dirty Harry fame. The stock currently trades near $11.00 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $475 million. The company has an unusual fiscal year that begins on April 1st.

Recent Results:

Smith & Wesson Brands posted its Q2 numbers on December 5th. The company delivered non-GAAP earnings of 11 cents a share, six cents a share under expectations. GAAP net income was $4.1 million, or $0.09 per diluted share. This was up from $2.5 million, or $0.05 per diluted share, in the same period a year ago. Gross margins rose 120bps to 26.6%.

Revenues rose just under four percent on a year-over-year basis to $129.7 million, which missed the consensus by nearly $4 million. Management noted several things about revenues during the quarter. The first was that the primary driver of weak demand continued to be inflation, which is lowering discretionary spending among many customers. However, leadership also noted demand “normalized” late in the quarter.

Management also stated that 44% of overall sales was driven by recent product launches, which appears encouraging. Average selling prices did drop by eight percent from the same period a year. Part of that was due to increase promotional activity to move inventory. However, some of was due to the launch of the Bodyguard 2.0 chambered in 380 ACP. This has quickly become one of the most purchased concealed pistols in the United States but also carries an average selling price of just $400.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Despite a decent size market cap, Smith & Wesson Brands gets little attention from Wall Street firms. Since second quarter results hit the wires, Craig-Hallum immediately downgraded the shares to a Hold and reduced its price target from $18 to $13 a share with the following commentary:

Weak consumer demand and a lackluster firearm-buying frenzy into this election cycle contributed to mixed results and a large cut to FY25 guidance. In our view, the firearms market has entered a digestion period following years of increased buying patterns (stricter regulation fears in early 2010s, pandemic in early 2020s)"

On January 27th, Lake Street upgraded the shares to a Buy from a Hold with the identical $13 a share price target.

Approximately three percent of the outstanding float in the stock is held short. Insider activity was limited in 2024, insiders sold just over $550,000 worth of stock last year collectively via five transactions during the year. There has been no insider activity in the stock so far in 2025.

During the second quarter of the company's fiscal 2025 year, management authorized a new $50 million share repurchase authorization. The company bought back $9.8 million of its stock during the quarter at an average price of just under $13.00 a share. The company paid out $5.8 million worth of dividends. Smith & Wesson also signed a new unsecured $175 million line of credit. This served two purposes. First, it increased total available borrowings by $75 million. It also extended the company's debt maturities to October 2029. Now that the firm has completed its move to Tennessee, management sees capital expenditures of $25 million to $30 million.

The company listed approximately $39 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against just under $99 million in notes payable on the 10-Q it filed for the quarter. The company accrued nearly $2.1 million of interest expense in the quarter. Management stated on its second quarter earnings call that cash generation will now be under its original $75 million target for FY2025.

Conclusion:

Smith & Wesson Brands made 92 cents on nearly $536 million of revenue in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling to 40 cents a share as sales fall to $491 million in FY2025. They project earnings to rebound to 62 cents a share on seven percent revenue growth in FY2026.

The stock trades at 12 times trailing earnings. Profits are projected to be more than cut in half in FY2025 as sales fall eight percent, before rebounding in fiscal 2026. An investor is getting paid to wait through this earnings/sales downturn with a 4.8% dividend yield in should be noted. The stock is trading in the lower end of its historical price to sales ratio range.

That said, I wish management would use the cash using to buy back stock to paid down debt. This would reduce leverage and lower interest expenses. I had a similar complaint when I posted an analysis of Iridium Communications (IRDM) earlier in the week.

Liquidity in the options around the equity is quite solid. Therefore, I have a small position via covered call positions in Smith & Wesson while it goes through its current inflection year. This provides some solid downside protection and enhances the yield from the quarterly dividend payouts.

data.title: Valeo: Uncertainty Remains Main Theme Amid Weak Auto Demand And Uncertain EV Timelines
data.description:
The markets notoriously hate uncertainty, and ongoing uncertainty seems to be about the only certainty regarding the outlook for electric vehicle production and adoption over the next few years. Although EV adoption continues to rise globally, much of that is being led by domestic Chinese production and the outlooks for auto sales overall and EV sales in particular are still quite murky in both North America and Europe.

None of this is good news for Valeo SE (OTCPK:VLEEY) (OTCPK:VLEEF), as this leading French auto supplier has been counting on content wins and production ramps with its EV content to drive both improved sales growth and margin leverage in the back half of this decade.

Valeo shares haven’t done badly since my last update, rising about 10% and outperforming most other auto parts suppliers (apart from Forvia (OTCPK:FAURY)). I believe some of this could have been driven by bargain-hunting, as well as prospects for improved share for EV OEMs competing with Tesla (TSLA), and the shares do remain significantly undervalued, but I believe it will take improved clarity on EV timelines to see an extended re-rating here.

Auto Demand – 'Less Bad' Is Not Exactly The Same As 'Good'

Last year was not a particularly strong one for global auto sales and overall production volumes closed the year down a little more than 1%, with fourth quarter numbers coming in better than expected. China drove what strength there was, with the U.S. roughly flat and Europe down around 5%.

At this point, 2025 isn’t looking particularly strong, with the S&P expecting global volumes down slightly (about 0.5%), leading by roughly 3% declines in the U.S. and Europe offset by further growth in China. For their part, companies like BorgWarner (BWA), Lear (LEA), Magna International (MGA), and PHINIA (PHIN) seem to broadly agree with this view, with guidance more or less corroborating those estimates.

While much of the Valeo story rides on the success of its recently developed components for hybrid and EV powertrains, the company still remains a diversified supplier of many automotive components (traditional, hybrid, and EV powertrains; climate and temperature management; lighting; internal controls; and driver assistance (including ADAS)) to multiple markets, so the global outlook for auto sales matters, and particularly for the European market that still contributes more than 40% of the company’s sales.

Unfortunately, I don’t see a lot of strong positive drivers in the near term. Increased protectionism could harm the company’s business in the U.S. (roughly 20% of sales), as well as other U.S.-specific issues (like the risk of weak consumer confidence undermining vehicle demand). Likewise, I don’t see a lot of strong drivers for demand in the EU at present, and increased protectionism (let alone an outright trade war) won’t help.

Share gains and new product/contract wins can and should help. Valeo has been gaining share in Europe and new contracts that go active in 2025 and beyond should help. Moreover, the ongoing expansion of enhanced ADAS, advanced lighting, and infotainment offerings in new models should be a tailwind for the company’s conventional offerings.

EV Uncertainty Remains A Key Issue

As I said above, the uncertain path of EV adoption outside of China remains a key headwind for Valeo as a business and a stock. Valeo has invested considerable sums in building up its low-voltage and high-voltage product offerings, and the company needs to see volume growth in the EV space to start earning meaningful returns on those investments.

This remains a “good news/bad news” market. While EV sales have been disappointing in North America and Europe, they are still growing, and global BEV sales were up 13% in December 2024 (and up 19% in terms of units). Moreover, sales should continue to grow at a double-digit rate, with penetration rates continuing to increase across Valeo’s markets. Furthermore, Tesla is not a major customer within Valeo’s EV business and if Tesla continues to lose share to other EV OEMs (particularly in Europe), it should benefit Valeo.

Still, there are issues to consider. First, the U.S. is almost certainly going to become less hospitable for EVs in the near term, with the new administration likely to repeal more stringent environmental regulations for new passenger vehicles and dismantle many of the incentive programs for electric vehicles. At the same time, while sales of EVs in China continue to grow well, there has been a meaningful share shift toward domestic producers, most of whom rely on domestic suppliers (Geely Automobile Holdings (OTCPK:GELYY) being a notable exception and meaningful Valeo customer).

At this point, there have been very few meaningful cancellations of major EV contracts, but there have been pushouts and that delays the manufacturing and operating leverage that Valeo shareholders have been anticipating for a few years now. Still, there remains a risk that major OEMs continue to backpedal from their EV launch and production plans. Offsetting this risk to at least some extent, Valeo remains a strong player in hybrid systems, and it seems unlikely that auto OEMs (particularly in Europe) will entirely abandon electrification, even if a move back towards hybrids as a “transitional technology” would lead to further delays in the volume/leverage ramp that Valeo needs.

The Outlook - Muted Expectations Are Likely A Plus Now

The best I can say about the near-term outlook for Valeo is that expectations have come down substantially over the last couple of years.

Valeo is due to report fourth quarter/second half 2024 results at the end of the month (February 27), and analysts are expecting a 6% year-over-year decline in revenue for the quarter, with flat adjusted operating margin (at 4.5%) and a modest improvement in EBITDA margin (up about 40bp to 12.8%).

While I’m not inclined to try to “polish the turd” and argue that these are strong results, I do like the fact that Valeo is making progress on its cost-cutting and efficiency efforts even with weak volumes weighing on operating leverage. I do see some possibility of modest upside to revenue, but it’s well worth watching how the company’s sales compare to underlying production volumes in its major operating areas, as share losses in North America and China are a concern.

Given how U.S.-based auto suppliers have guided for 2025 so far, there’s likely more downside risk than upside to Valeo’s 2025 guidance, but I do think 3% year-over-year growth is at least possible, as well as further EBITDA leverage. Longer term, I continue to expect around 4% long-term revenue growth as the company gains share on the back of hybrid and EV powertrains, further expansion of ADAS, and growth in areas like software-defined vehicle components and software.

On the margin side, I still think it’s going to take a few years of grinding before there is strong leverage in margins and FCF generation. I still expect EBITDA margins to move toward 15% over the next five years, with FCF margins moving to 2% and then toward 2.5% over time, but Valeo needs a healthy environment for auto sales/production and further acceleration in hybrid and fully electric vehicle sales and the timing is still very uncertain.

Looking at valuation, expectations remain exceptionally low. Working backwards from today’s valuation and using a double-digit valuation, the shares would still be modestly undervalued (around 10% to 20%) if the company only grows revenue at around 2% a year (roughly in line with developed market volume growth expectations) and sees no margin/FCF generation leverage relative to its norms over the last five years.

Suffice it to say, if my expectations of 4% revenue growth and 2%-2.5% FCF margins are at least in the right zip code (let alone the right ballpark), the shares are substantially undervalued. Likewise, if I use a shorter-term margin-driven EV/revenue approach. While the auto sector in general continues to trade near historical lows on this approach, a 40% discount to those norms still gets me to a 0.5x forward EV/revenue ratio, and that’s more than double where the shares are now.

The Bottom Line

Even with recent strength in the share price performance, expectations for Valeo are dismally low today. I can’t say that’s entirely undeserved given the challenges in the EV sector and overall demand for cars in developed markets, but it does seem excessive. I do still think these shares remain significantly undervalued for the long term, but I’m staying “neutral” for my short-term rating, as I do think the market will need a little more assurance on auto sales trends (and EVs in particular), as well as Valeo’s margin progression, before these shares really re-rate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cohu, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cohu, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: IPG Photonics Corporation: A Further Decline Is Still Possible
data.description:
IPG Photonics Corporation (NASDAQ:IPGP), a leading manufacturer of fiber lasers, released its fourth quarterly report for FY2024 on February 11. In general, FY2024 was a year to forget as it saw sales and profits contract, the third consecutive year to do so. The year also ended on a weak note with the Q4 FY2024 report missing earnings expectations, on top of weaker-than-expected guidance. The stock price has been heading lower during most of the past year and, with the latest report, the stock may be going for new lows, which would be in line with the existing trend. Why will be covered next.

What the stock does next could be telling

A previous article from August 2024 rated IPGP a hold after concluding that even though the stock had already accumulated substantial losses, the possibility the stock may have further to drop could not be excluded for a number of reasons. IPGP, for instance, faces a number of headwinds, which seem structural in nature and for which IPGP has yet to find a definitive solution.

The stock also lost support, which had held for a long time. More often than not, the more time the stock spent getting past support, as in this case, the bigger the downwards move that follows. This suggested that with the stock still fresh from falling below support, the stock had further to go before it was worth considering getting in.

The chart above showed why the article initially appeared to have drawn the wrong conclusions, as the stock moved higher, only for the article to be shown to be on the right track more recently, with the stock going down as the article thought it could. The stock first fell to new lows in September, only to rally higher in the following months, but the stock then proceeded to move lower to the point that the stock price is now below where it was when the aforementioned article was written.

If we look closer at the chart, we see that the stock is currently very close to where it previously bottomed in September in the $60-65 region, before the rally that followed. The stock bottomed at $62.31 on September 10, and it got as low of $61.86 if intraday lows are included. This is not far from where the stock closed at on February 12 at $62.97. The stock actually hit a new multi-year low of $60.72 on an intraday basis.

The fact that the stock closed much higher off the lows could be seen by the bulls as a positive sign, especially if the stock builds on the bounce in the coming days. It might even cause some to believe the stock is going for a repeat of what happened in September. Remember that the stock closed off the lows at the bottom in September, which heralded the start of a rally.

What the stock does next could be telling for those who are bullish, or alternatively bearish. Keep in mind that the stock is now in oversold territory with an RSI reading in the twenties. If the stock proceeds to bounce at this point, whether it is due to being oversold or what not, traders could interpret the stock bouncing at roughly the same price point in the $60-65 region as a double bottom, which is regarded as a bullish signal.

So if there is a bounce soon, this might encourage more buyers to step in, which may be enough to trigger another rally like what happened last September. On the other hand, the absence of a bounce is likely to further strengthen the conviction of the bears, especially since the existing trend favors the bears.

The chart above shows how the stock has moved lower in recent years. Notice how the high is at the extreme left and the low is at the extreme right, which is how anyone bearish on a stock would like it to be. IPGP’s market cap, for instance, is less than a quarter of what it was back in 2021 at the peak. The trend favors lower stock prices, although that does not rule out the possibility the stock might break the trend. Yet placing bets on such an outcome could be a case of trying to catch a falling knife.

Why IPGP fell to new lows after the latest report

It’s no coincidence IPGP hit a new multi-year low after the release of the Q4 FY2024 report in recent days because the report was worse than expected. For instance, the market expected GAAP EPS of $0.33 on revenue of $235M in Q1 FY2025, but guidance from IPGP calls for EPS of $0.05-0.35 on revenue of $210-240M, both down YoY, as shown in the table below. Keep in mind this comes against a backdrop in which IPGP has seen quarterly sales contract for 10 consecutive quarters and Q1 FY2025 guidance suggests there will be a number 11.

(GAAP)

Q1 FY2025 (guidance)

Q1 FY2024

YoY (midpoint)

Sales

$210-240M

$252.0M

(10.71%)

Gross margin

36-39%

38.7%

(120bps)

EPS

$0.05-0.35

$0.52

(61.54%)

Source: IPGP Form 8-K

On top of the latest guidance were Q4 FY2024 earnings that came in mixed. The consensus expected GAAP EPS of $0.20 on revenue of $227.4M, but IPGP earned $0.18 on revenue of $234.3M, down 79.8% and 21.6% YoY, respectively. Note that the large loss of $233.6M or $5.33 a share in Q3 FY2024 was due to the divestiture of IPGP’s operations in Russia.

The table below shows why FY2024 was a year to forget. Sales declined by 24.1% YoY to $977.1M and the year ended with a net loss of $181.5M or $4.09 a share, primarily because of the Q3 FY2024 loss mentioned earlier. Book-to-bill ended below one. Note how the number of outstanding shares decreased by 6.3% YoY due to IPGP spending $344M in FY2024 to buy back shares, including $57M in the last quarter. IPGP finished with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments totaling $930.19M on the balance sheet with no debt.

A company needs to have earned a net profit in order to have a P/E, which IPGP did not do in terms of GAAP. However, if we were to exclude the negative impact of the unit sale in Russia and assume FY2024 net income would be higher by about $225M without the sale, then FY2024 net income could have been about $43.5M or $0.98 a share. This would have given IPGP a P/E ratio of 64.3x with the stock at $62.97.

This is more than what most stocks go for. On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that most earnings estimates on the Street predict an increase in FY2025 EPS compared to FY2024. Estimates vary greatly and there is a wide range to be found, but if we take the average, then we get something around $2.20, which translates to a forward P/E of 28.6x.

How IPGP tried to cushion the impact of the most recent results

IPGP seemed to have realized that the latest numbers are not encouraging to say the least, which is probably why it felt the need to try to negate any blowback by focusing on the future outlook. For instance, IPGP pointed how there are market opportunities out there that could help it return to growth in the not too distant future, likely in an effort to cushion the release of a disappointing report. From the Q4 earnings call:

“And the key areas that I'm talking about there are urology, micro-machining, advanced applications. And these are programs that the target market that are markets exceeding $5 billion in TAM and offer IPG really hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue opportunities over the next several years. And it's important to also mention that we're -- in these areas, we're working collaboratively with key customers and we already have purchase orders in place for some of these programs.

We expect to see benefits from these later this year and really kicking in 2026 and '27.”

Source: IPGP earnings call

Is there reason for caution?

Keep in mind that forward estimates are subject to possible downwards revisions since they could turn out to be too optimistic as the year progresses. The estimates predict growth will start to pick up, but this is no sure thing. It is not impossible for IPGP to come up short, which is what already happened with the latest guidance.

IPGP is still facing headwinds on a number of fronts. In the latest report, sales declined in all the major regions with China down 22%, Europe by 22% and North America by 31%. There is also reason to believe further decreases are very possible in China and Europe. The manufacturing sector in Europe is in a state of crisis due to the cost of energy, which is almost certain to continue in 2025.

In the case of China, competition is reducing IPGP’s share of the market with Chinese OEMs offering products that compete against those of IPGP. IPGP seems to believe the pressure is confined to cutting and it is counting on the introduction of new products like high-powered lasers in a more compact form, or by focusing on end markets like medical, to counter the competition, but there is nothing stopping competitors from also upgrading their offerings.

IPGP seems to believe its experience working with fiber lasers accumulated over the years will prove decisive versus competitors, but it is not impossible for an upstart to overtake established players in tech. There are many examples throughout history of tech companies that were overtaken by new arrivals, even though the former were around for much longer. It is an advantage, but not something that cannot be overcome.

Investor takeaways

IPGP may have tried to strike an upbeat tone to offset disappointing guidance, but it did not stop the stock from falling to new lows. Business continues to contract, but IPGP is optimistic about its ability to return to growth in its outlook for the coming years. However, the market seemed to have paid more attention to the present, which shows IPGP is a company that is dealing with problems it has yet to solve, because the stock price fell to levels it last saw in 2014.

It’s also worth reminding readers that optimism on the part of IPGP has turned out to be premature before. For example, IPGP once thought growth could return in the second half of FY2024, which did not pan out as shown in the last report for FY2024. This probably played a role as to why the optimistic outlook from IPGP, especially as it relates to future opportunities in FY2026 and FY2027, failed to give the stock much of a lift.

Granted, IPGP’s strategy of enabling a turnaround in falling sales does stand a chance of success. On paper, new products could foster a return to growth, but it will have to fend off growing competition, which was not there in prior years. IPGP pioneered the practical use of high-powered fiber lasers, which gave it a leading position in the market with few credible competitors, but the latter looks increasingly like something from the past. IPGP will have to put up with a bigger fight, something it did not really have to do in the past.

What the stock does in the short term could be telling. The stock is oversold and if the stock bounces where it did back in September, then this might trigger a rally by convincing speculators to jump in. But no bounce would open the door to new lows. It would also show the stock remains very much in the downtrend it has been in for years really. Note also there is room for a temporary bounce without changing the overall trend, provided the rally is kept contained.

The stock is at a point where speculators might try to roll the dice because of oversold conditions and the charts, which could make a bounce possible, but I am neutral on IPGP. Trends do change at some point, but to bet against the existing trend requires some solid argument as to why. It is difficult to make such an argument with IPGP yet to resolve the challenges it faces, particularly in China and Europe, as shown once again by the most recent report.

Bottom line, the trend is your friend and the trend suggests there are likely lower stock prices in the pipeline. The ongoing problems in Europe and China are supportive of this trend staying in place. If long IPGP is to be considered, the downtrend has to be broken first. This is not likely to happen in the short term with the way the cards are presently laid out.

data.title: Interfor: Prices Are Rationalizing, But With New Uncertainties (Downgrade)
data.description:
Interfor Corporation (OTCPK:IFSPF) logged in an alright fourth quarter. Prices have come up sequentially, though volumes are actually a little down. The reality is that there really isn't demand coming online just because of some initial rate hikes as we imagined in our previous coverage. There is still some apprehensiveness about the economy. For Q4, there was still the uncertainty of the incoming presidency. Based on the call, it appears that the environment continues to be cautious as issues like tariffs, though not an overwhelming problem for Interfor, are likely a worry for homebuilders. But price improvements were evident, showing that the industry is rationalizing at least a little bit now.

While it's high time for a recovery in the homebuilding market, we do worry about another geopolitical factor. That is the possibility of falling log costs in Europe if the Ukraine war ends, which could increase the competitiveness of European exports. Long-term rates related to re-inflation are also a risk factor. Too complex to consider for us.

Latest Earnings

The general attitude in the Q4 earnings call was that there hasn't been a great signal of recovery. In fact, even compared to last quarter, management felt that the demand outlook was in the short-term, that is to say on a week-to-week basis, looking as if it's declining even currently in the running Q1. Apparently, activity in distribution channels is at historical lows. For the fourth quarter, there have at least been some constructive movements in price on a YOY and QoQ basis. EBITDA also improved significantly sequentially and YOY as the industry, Interfor included as number #2 in North America in terms of market share in lumber, wound down some mills here and there and/or reduced shifts. A help was also a bit of a glut that attenuated log prices due to storms in the U.S. This forced more than normal amounts of logs into the market, with salvage efforts underway in the Southeast. Interfor's operations are concentrated there.

Latent in the Interfor picture is the fact that there really should come a point where homebuilding demand recovers. Housing starts have been worse in the recent past, with December actually seeing a bit of a boost. But this has probably not been sustained into Q1, based on management's comments around the lack of real homebuilding demand recovery. Some reasons why starts are not picking up enough to bolster demand is on the one hand due to general economic concerns, but also geopolitical developments and domestic political developments like tariffs that seriously influence the decisions of homebuilders.

Bottom Line

The economic considerations would be around rates. Low rates are needed to bolster demand. Rates came down a couple of times in 2024, but it's clear that the Fed is not seeing enough disinflation at this point, actually, any disinflation at all, to be able to permit further cuts. It is widely and conventionally considered at this point that the comprehensive policies of the Trump administration are an inflationary vector, so the yield curve has de-inverted and the forward view on rates is not so favorable for housing.

The threatened tariffs on Canada, held off now for a month while a deal can be worked out, could affect 25% of Interfor's volumes of Canadian lumber coming into the U.S. Canada has gone down in the mix for Interfor due to beetle infestations over the past few years but also disposals of some operations in Quebec. Still, in the current results, we estimate that accounts for around 2% of lumber production, improving that 25% to around 23%.

Another issue is European exports to the U.S. When export via the Red Sea became an issue, we covered Interfor over the issue that this could divert more production to the U.S. and extend the rationalization process of the U.S.'s lumber markets. This did happen, as European exports into the U.S. did jump in that period after a sustained decline from the lumber demand peaks in 2021-2022. We speculate that with a deal to end the Ukraine war clearly the objective of the Trump administration, possible consequences could be somewhat recovered trade flows between Russia and the rest of Europe. Russian boreal timber is a major needle-mover for overall European supply. The reintroduction of Russian timber in the market could drive log and fiber costs down. For European lumber, this could mean the ability to price more competitively. Homebuilders even in the U.S. may be waiting on lower prices of lumber from another wave of European competition. On the other hand, maybe Europe gets slapped with tariffs — too early to tell.

While there are these risks, there are also the benefits of an inelastic lumber supply, at least to the upside, where incremental production increases even in the high-demand period of COVID-19 was only in the single digits. This feature of the industry also contributes to its general volatility, conversely, the potential for significant increases in lumber prices if demand were there for it. As the expected tailwinds materialize from at least somewhat lower rates (though long-term rates are not looking helpful right now), secularly from demographics and lagging overall home supply even against current population figures, lumber plays could benefit. However, we think that the risks particularly around the competitiveness of European logs (absent tariffs) are meaningful. Uncertainty makes this a wait-and-see idea for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Moncler: Critical Phase Is Over, But It's No Longer A Buy
data.description:
In late 2024, I published a series of buy articles on Moncler (OTCPK:MONRF), an Italian haute couture company that was collapsing due to the tightening economic environment. The entire haute couture industry was in trouble (and still is), but after only a few months Moncler seems to have already made up for lost ground.

The annual report released yesterday was a success on all fronts, and the market rewarded Moncler with a gain of 7%. Anyway, the stock was already on the rise, and today at $70.62 per share I no longer think it is a buy. In my opinion this company is among the best in the world in its industry, which is why I have included it in my portfolio, but it may take some time before I increase my position. I have enough invested around $55 per share, and I don't think it is worth increasing my average buy price.

Highlights FY2024/Q4 2024

As you know, the main problem in this industry has been China's economic meltdown. Luxury goods have a very high demand in the east, and with the Chinese real estate crisis, the situation has changed. However, in Q4 2024, Moncler showed encouraging signs of growth, and analysts are beginning to be more optimistic about an economic recovery for the Chinese. If China fully recovers from the real estate crisis, so will the luxury industry. This explains why the market is so bullish on Moncler while a few months ago no one was considering it.

Let us now look in detail at the performance of the company's two main brands, Moncler and Stone Island.

Moncler had an 8% increase in revenues on both an annual and quarterly basis. What is important to note here is the increasing share that Asia is getting on the whole portfolio. Moncler has actually never really been in trouble in this respect, unlike competitors like LVMH and Kering, which is why I considered it a buy. As of today, however, with the China situation improving, revenues are growing double digits again in Asia: +11% vs. Q4 2023 and FY2023.

According to management, this positive result was due to a series of choices and investments made in China in recent years; so it is not just a matter of the country's economic recovery. Moncler knows Chinese consumers well and is helped by a local team to understand their needs. It also holds incredible events throughout the year to increase its visibility, such as Moncler Genius in Shanghai.

If Moncler has been resilient to China's adverse economic environment, it is because its competitive advantage in this country is enormous. When the Chinese think of a high-quality brand for winter, Moncler is the first one that comes to their mind: there are no real competitors in outerwear.

And I think we have been able to develop a trust and quality perception of Moncler that is very, very high in the market. But this is not the only element. I think we have also developed. We are one of the few brands where we – or maybe even the only go to brand in China for luxury outerwear. So really, when the call is coming and when people thinking about full winter, we are absolutely top of mind. In the – and there is not a real competitor in the market, at least not in our positioning.

Roberto Eggs – Chief Business Strategy and Global Market Officer

It is quite ironic how analysts have been concerned about Asia, but it is Americas and EMEA that are the slowest growing business segments. In particular, EMEA grew only 3% on a quarterly basis, while on an annual basis it improved by 5%: meaning that growth in EMEA is struggling. Italy was the country in EMEA with the best growth, but it seems that the main problem concerns a lower turnout of Chinese tourists in EMEA.

We still are missing some of the tourists we had in the past, especially in Europe because, yes, there is a presence of Chinese in Europe, but absolutely not to the extent that we had before, there is a 10% gap in the weight of Chinese in Europe, in 2024 compared to 2019.

Roberto Eggs – Chief Business Strategy and Global Market Officer

When the economic situation in China improves, perhaps there will be more Chinese tourists and Moncler can benefit.

In terms of distribution channels, Moncler is doing an excellent job. The goal is to increase DTC sales, as they involve higher profit margins and greater brand exclusivity. The latter aspect is crucial for high-fashion companies. DTC sales in FY2024 reached 86%, which is 2% higher than last year.

Let us now look at the performance of Stone Island, a very interesting brand but one that has so far disappointed expectations somewhat.

On an annual basis they have disappointed in EMEA and Americas sales, declining 7% and 18% respectively. However, in Q4 2024 they showed a low-mid single-digit recovery and this is promising. In Asia both quarterly and annual growth rates were exceptional, +23% despite a difficult economic environment.

The company is betting heavily on Stone Island and investment will continue to elevate this brand. It may have the potential of Moncler, but you first have to stable a mass culture behind it, and that is not something you can create overnight.

Another allocation of resources and capital is something that we plan to develop on the retail network. But as you know, right now, we want to first to fully to properly develop what we call the retail culture in the existing stores to bring the culture that has developed over the years in Moncler to develop the same culture in Stone Island to make the existing stores more and more productive to work on all the different metrics of retail. And again, it's something that requires a culture that takes time.

Luciano Santel – Chief Corporate and Supply Officer

At the moment, the priority is not to expand Stone Island as much as possible by opening new stores, but to improve the profitability of existing stores. On the other hand, the wholesale distribution channel has declined sharply in favor of the retail channel.

In 2023, 58% of sales were wholesale, by the end of 2024 only 48%. The retail channel is becoming increasingly important and is part of the strategy to elevate the brand.

Overall, the performance of 2024 was quite positive. In fact, revenues increased by 4% while maintaining a very high profit margin of 20.60%. A lot is always expected from such a company and mid-single digit growth may seem disappointing, but you have to put these figures in context with the environment in which Moncler operated in 2024. Only Hermès managed double-digit growth; major brands such as Gucci saw a 23% drop in sales.

Moncler has proven to be a reliable company even in adversity. After all, cash exceeds debt, the competitive advantage is huge especially in China, and free cash flow continues to grow.

In FY 2024 it reached €587 million, up 7% from last year. The cash generated was enough to increase the dividend and further improve the net financial position. But again, this is considered a bad year for haute couture and Moncler can definitely do better in the coming years.

After this sharp rise, Moncler's PE Ratio rose to 28x, not too high for such a company, but I would wait for a retracement to consider it a buy again. Anyway, I have full confidence in its very long-term performance, which is why buying it now is not totally nonsense. My thinking might be influenced by the fact that I bought it a few months ago at a price 20% lower.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Agios Pharmaceuticals: Liver Toxicity Issues May Threaten Thalassemia Approval (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

Agios Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:AGIO), the Cambridge, Massachusetts based biotech, developer of Pyrukund (mitapivat), "an activator of both wild-type and mutant pyruvate kinase, or PK, enzymes for the potential treatment of hemolytic anemias", announced its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings yesterday.

The company earned $10.7m of revenues in Q4, and made a net loss of $(96.5m), thanks to R&D expenses of $82.8m, and SG&A expenses of $51.7m. For the full-year, revenues were $36.5m, up from $26.8m in the prior year, cost of sales $4.2m, R&D expenses $301m, and SG&A expenses $157m.

Agios reported net income of $674m, however, thanks to a "gain on sale of contingent payments". This relates back to the sale of its oncology division - including isocitrate dehydrogenase-1 (IDH1) inhibitor Tibsovo (vorasidenib), approved in 2017 to treat myeloma, to Servier Pharmaceuticals for $1.8bn back in April 2021.

In August 2024, vorasidenib was approved to treat astrocytoma (a form of brain cancer), and Agios received a $200m milestone payment from Servier as part of the terms of its sale, while the approval also meant Agios was able to complete the sale of its royalty rights in relation to vorasidenib to Royalty Pharma (RPRX), for $905m in cash.

As a result of all this, Agios ended 2024 with a very health cash position of $1.5bn, up from $806.m at the end of 2023.

Pyrukund Agios' New Priority As It Seeks To Expand Label

Agios' focus ever since selling its oncology business has been on Pyrukund, a drug for which it secured approval in the indication of hemolytic anemia in adults with pyruvate kinase ("PK") deficiency back in 2022. Pyrukynd tablets are taken orally twice daily with or without food.

As Agios' revenues imply, Pyrukund's current indication is not a lucrative one, even if revenues in Q4 were up >50% year-on-year, but the company's ambition - which is inching closer to being realised - is to secure approvals for its lead drug in the indications of thalassemia and sickle cell disease ("SCD") - which are multi-billion dollar markets.

Thalassemia is an inherited blood disorder that causes your body to have less hemoglobin than normal, and can cause anemia and extreme fatigue. Agios estimates (in its 2024 annual report / 10K submission) that the disease affects 18k -23k individuals in the US, with ~6k diagnosed patients, ~70k people in Gulf Council countries ("GCC"), and potentially 1m people worldwide.

In sickle cell disease, red blood cells are misshaped, or "sickle" shaped, and can block blood flow as a result, leading to pain crises which often require medical attention or hospitalisation. This is a 120k - 135k patient market in the US, ~150k in GCC, and >3m worldwide.

Pivot, Progress Towards Approvals, & Share Price Turbulence

In June 2021, shortly after the sale of its oncology business was agreed, Agios stock was worth >$60 per share, however by May 2022, it had sunk to a value of <$20, despite Pyrukund's approval in PK deficiency - suggesting the market was less than impressed with Agios' new direction.

Members of senior management left and Agios announced lay-offs and a plan to pivot towards genetic diseases, and appointed a new CEO, Brian Goff, however at the beginning of 2024 shares still traded <$20.

Then, Agios shared positive data from its Phase 3 study of mitapivat in transfusion dependent alpha or beta thalassemia, in which the drug met its primary endpoint, as:

42.3% of patients in the mitapivat arm achieved a hemoglobin response, compared to 1.6% of patients in the placebo arm (2-sided p<0.0001).

Treatment with mitapivat also demonstrated statistically significant improvements compared to placebo for both key secondary endpoints:

  • Change from baseline in average FACIT-Fatigue (Functional Assessment of Chronic Illness Therapy-Fatigue) subscale score from Week 12 to Week 24.
  • Change from baseline in average hemoglobin concentration from Week 12 to Week 24.

After the data were announced, I initiated my coverage of Agios on Seeking Alpha, giving the stock a "Buy" rating - shares are up 48% since that note (at the time of writing).

News of the deal with Royalty Pharma also buoyed Agios' share price, which reached $48 per share in July, and the news that Pfizer's SCD drug Oxbryta, which had earned $328m of revenues in 2023, had been withdraw from the market due to safety issues also seemed to drive upside (despite some analysts concerns that mitapivat could meet a similar fate).

By early December Agios stock had reached a value of >$60 per share, its highest value since 2019, seemingly vindicating the biotech's decision to pursue a new direction, but then the company released its latest Phase 3 thalassemia data, triggering a major market sell-off, and dropping the share price back >$35.

Two patients in the study had experienced liver injury following treatment with mitapivat, the data revealed. Agios disclosed in a press release:

Based on the data from the ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T studies, Agios included, in its regulatory applications, hepatocellular injury as an important potential risk of mitapivat in patients with thalassemia and proposed monthly monitoring of liver tests for the first six months of treatment with mitapivat. In addition, mitapivat clinical trial protocols across all indications have been updated to incorporate similar monitoring.

In January, Agios announced that the Food and Drug Agency ("FDA") had accepted its supplemental New Drug Application ("sNDA") for Pyrukund to treat adult patients with non-transfusion-dependent and transfusion-dependent alpha- or beta-thalassemia, and assigned a Prescription Drug User Fee Act ("PDUFA") date - or decision date, in more straightforward terms, of September 7th, 2025.

Meanwhile, Agios stated yesterday in its earning press release that enrollment in its Phase 3 RISE UP study in Sickle Cell Disease had been completed, and that topline data would likely be shared towards the end of 2025.

Analysis - Market Opportunity

We can break down the investment opportunity in relation to Agios today in relatively simple terms. Assuming Agios wins approval for Pyrukund in thalassemia, and sets a list price similar to the price of Oxbryta prior to its withdrawal - ~$125k - and if we use the figure of 6k diagnosed patients - there is a market opportunity of ~$750m in play. If we include undiagnosed patients, that opportunity may rise as high as $2.5bn.

Agios' current market cap is $1.9bn. Besides Pyrukund, approved drugs to treat thalassemia include the gene editing drugs Casgevy, developed by Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) and Vertex (VRTX), and Zyntelgo, developed by Bluebird Bio (BLUE). I would not consider these therapies rivals of Pyrukund, however, as they are expensive, offer a permanent cure, and treatment can take a year or more, hence, only transfusion dependent patients would likely consider them.

Reblozyl (lustapercept), indicated to treat anemia caused by either myelodysplastic syndromes ("MDS") or thalassemia, might be considered the main market rival - the drug earned >$1.5bn in revenues in 2024 - however it too is indicated for patients wo require regular transfusions.

If we assume a commercial stage biotech typically trades at ~5x sales, assuming it is not heavily loss-making, and that based on the available evidence, Pyrukund has "blockbuster" potential (>$1bn revenues per annum), then we can get to a valuation of ~$5bn for Agios, or >150% higher than today's market cap valuation.

Incidentally, when Pfizer bought out Global Blood Therapeutics, who marketed and sold Oxbryta prior to its withdrawal (what a disastrous deal for Pfizer that was), the deal was valued at $5.4bn.

We should also note that an approval in SCD - pending positive data in 2025 could double or even triple the addressable market / revenue opportunity for Pyrukund, with minimal competition in the market presently.

So far the bull case is taking shape, but what about the safety issues? If pyrukund is rejected for approval, this will likely have a devastating effect on the company's share price. There is a back-up plan in the form of pipeline candidate Tebapivat, a "next-generation" mitapivat, being evaluated in SCD and MDS, but it remains in early stage studies.

We should also note the competition - Agios is not the only company eyeing up the thalassemia / SCD space - according to Agios' 10K submission:

recently approved treatments for thalassemia, SCD, LR MDS, PKU and PV include Reblozyl from Merck (MRK)/BMS (BMY); Revlimid® from BMS; Zynteglo and Lyfgenia from bluebird; Adakveo from Novartis (NVS); Casgevy from Vertex/CRISPR; Kuvan and Palynziq from BioMarin (BMRN); Endari from Emmaus; Besremi from PharmaEssentia; Jakafi from Incyte; and Rytelo from Geron (GERN).

I've discussed reblozyl and the gene editing drugs, but seemingly, Adakveo, Endari, Besremi, and Jakafi could all be pivoted into potential thalassemia or SCD drugs, or be prescribed off-label.

Concluding Thoughts - Based on Available Evidence Is Agios Stock A "Buy", "Sell", or "Hold"?

Personally, I believe it all comes down to the safety question. Clearly, had it not been for the instances of hepatocellular injury observed, the market would still be valuing Agios stock at ~$60 per share, or a market cap of ~$4bn. In its 10K Agios notes:

during the double-blind periods of ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T, two patients on PYRUKYND® experienced events of hepatocellular injury. In addition, during the open-label extension periods of both trials, a total of three patients experienced events of hepatocellular injury after switching from placebo to PYRUKYND®. All of these events occurred within the first six months of exposure to PYRUKYND® and liver tests improved following discontinuation of PYRUKYND

Seemingly, then, it was not just two cases of hepatocellular injury, but potentially as many as five. Agios's solution is for patients to be monitored closely during their first six months of Pyrukund, and for treatment to be discontinued if liver injury is observed. Agios says the safety events were:

characterized by a time to onset within the first 6 months of treatment with peak elevations of alanine aminotransferase of >5× upper limit of normal (ULN) with or without jaundice. All patients discontinued treatment with PYRUKYND, and these events improved upon treatment discontinuation.

The liver injuries were potentially life-threatening, even if they improved when use of mitapivat was stopped, and occurred in <2% of all patients receiving the drug in the study period. This is a different issue to that which saw Oxbryta withdrawn - SCD patients were experiencing more pain crises, and more deaths in the treatment group than in the placebo group.

According to a paper I found online shared by the National Library of Medicine:

ALT levels of 5 to 10 times the upper limit of normal (~200 to 400 U/L) without symptoms or jaundice cannot be considered severe hepatotoxicity, and should instead be referred to as “Grade 3 ALT elevations.

As such, I don't see this issue derailing the thalassemia approval push, but it could lead to an unwanted "Black Box" warnings, and make physicians more reluctant to prescribe the drug. Otherwise, mitapivat appeared to a have a decent safety profile - apparently, in the pivotal thalassemia study, "serious treatment-emergent adverse events were reported in 11.0% (n=19) and 15.3% (n=13) of patients on mitapivat and placebo, respectively.

All things considered, then - the fact Pyrukund is already approved, the successful Phase 3, liver injury issues aside, the market opportunity, the paucity of other treatment options, the potential label expansion into Sickle Cell Disease, and the $1.5bn cash position, I'd expect an approval in September, quite possibly followed by a further approval in SCD in 2026.

With that said, the liver toxicity could substantially affect how widespread use of Pyrukund becomes. Reblozyl does not have a Black Box warning, for example, not that I am necessarily suggesting Pyrukund / mitapivat will have one.

In the end, I think it is really tricky to answer the question posed as to whether Agios stock is a "buy", "sell", or "hold, but given safety is the issue, I have to err on the side of caution and suggest a hold. It's possible - although Agios expressed its hope this does not happen - that the FDA will convene an Advisory Committee to discuss the safety issues and vote on whether they should prevent the approval in thalassemia.

In that scenario, we may see a further drop in Agios' share price, so I think its best to monitor progress from the sidelines for the time being, and until there is more clarity.

data.title: JEPI: Losing Its Appeal With This Major Flaw
data.description:
Since writing an article about the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) where I discussed how it was "no longer that attractive," I had received multiple requests for my opinion on the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI), which I will be sharing in this article. I went into my deep analysis of JEPI thoroughly expecting to write a positive article on the fund. Its performance in 2022 and 2023 was undeniably good and showed how the fund's strategy of actively selecting stocks to lower volatility and writing out-of-the-money ("OTM") calls on the S&P 500 via ELNs had led to superior performances during bear markets. In preparation for writing this article, however, deeper analysis suggests that JEPI isn't as good as people make it out to be, especially with the numerous alternative solutions on the market. First, the lower volatility and downside JEPI provides are largely determined by proprietary stock selection strategies that do not necessarily guarantee lower downsides. This is not a good combination with its covered call strategy, which caps JEPI's upsides and makes it more difficult to capture the much-needed recovery gains after large market drops. Distributions from JEPI's ELN options strategy are not particularly stable or desirable, too, as distributions can vary heavily based on volatility and are taxed largely as ordinary income. Although JEPI's performance has been good enough for many income investors and retirees, one should consider opportunity costs. Both GPIX and SPYI are ETFs I prefer over JEPI as they capture upside potential better and distribute more stable payments that reduce the need to lower share price volatility in the first place.

JEPI's Success In 2022

JEPI is an extremely popular fund among retirees due to its strategy of providing large monthly distributions and maintaining lower volatility compared to the overall market. It doesn't matter if JEPI underperforms the overall market in the long run. A key focus for many income investors is to generate income to support their retirement. Share price and capital appreciation are less of a concern so long as the overall portfolio is not going down in value. JEPI is a fund that fits this description, and it showed its worth during the 2022 bear market. JEPI had outperformed the S&P 500 in total returns between January 1, 2022, and December 31, 2023. Indeed, JEPI's volatility has also been consistently lower than SPY since its inception.

There are two fundamental components to JEPI's strategy that have helped the fund achieve these results. The first component is JEPI's active selection of stocks that lower volatility. What you'll notice when you first look at JEPI's holdings is that their sector concentrations are very different from the S&P 500. For example, technology only occupies 19.33% of JEPI's portfolio versus the 32.70% in SPY. This smaller concentration of technology in JEPI has been spread across the other sectors, leading to less sector risk.

Moreover, JEPI does not select all of the S&P 500's constituents and holds its stocks in more equal-weight positions. Its top holding currently, META, only occupies 1.80% of the portfolio versus the same stock taking a 3.08% position in SPY. Not to mention that SPY's top holding, AAPL, occupies nearly 7% of the portfolio. By taking more of an equal-weight approach, JEPI has managed to lower single-stock risk significantly.

The second component of JEPI's strategy that has helped lower its volatility significantly is its strategy of selling covered calls on the S&P 500 via ELNs. This is also the component that generates most of JEPI's monthly distribution. The reason selling covered calls can help lower volatility is that the option premiums generated by covered calls increase with volatility.

With these two components of active selection of lower volatility stocks and covered calls, you can see how JEPI navigated the 2022 bear market so well compared to the overall market. A more evenly-distributed portfolio among sectors in JEPI helped neutralize some of the big losses driven by tech and consumer discretionary stocks in the market cap-weighted S&P 500. Additionally, increased volatility led to increased option premiums from JEPI's ELNs, which offset losses significantly, with 2022 being JEPI's year with the biggest payouts.

In theory, JEPI is a fund that will drop less than the overall market while simultaneously increasing its payouts during downturns, and based on what we saw in 2022, this theory has shown to work so far. Why then, am I rating JEPI negatively?

The Importance of Capturing Upside In Covered Call Funds

The problem with JEPI is that downside protection is not guaranteed. When we look at JEPIX, the mutual fund that implements the same strategy as JEPI, we can see how the fund would have performed during the 2020 pandemic crash. What you'll see is that JEPIX crashed almost as much as SPY.

This becomes a significant concern when considering that the biggest risk with selling covered calls is the capped upside, especially on the recovery. Keep in mind that if your asset loses 20% in value, it'll take a 25% gain to recover to its original price. If JEPI can drop just as much as the S&P 500 but doesn't have the same growth prospects, it will naturally lead to long-term underperformance.

Inefficient, Variable Distributions

Of course, what's more important about JEPI is its monthly distributions. This is supposed to be a fund that retirees can buy without thinking much and live off the dividend payments. There are a few problems with this approach, though. While yields have typically ranged around 7% and as much as 12%, distribution amounts change frequently with volatility and JEPI's underlying holdings.

For reference, JEPI's distributions have a standard deviation level of 9.31%, while competitor funds like GPIX and SPYI have distribution standard deviation levels of 1.64% and 2.12%, respectively. In other words, you can frequently expect JEPI's distribution to change close to 10% every month on average. This brings up a big discussion point for retirees and income investors. Would you prefer to reduce volatility in your underlying portfolio or your income?

For most income investors, reducing volatility in income makes more sense than reducing volatility in the overall portfolio. So long as the portfolio values recover, having a stable income makes day-to-day financial planning far easier.

JEPI's distributions are also primarily ordinary income for those investing in taxable accounts, with small portions classified as qualified dividend income due to their ELNs. Essentially, those investing in non tax-sheltered accounts will find their variable distributions even more difficult to manage. Ordinary income taxes are typically the highest tax rates and with yields that can get as low as 6%, you are getting into territories where stocks paying qualified dividends can lead to more money in your pockets after taxes. If you make $60K a year, for example, 6.6% yielding VZ would lead to more money than a 7.16% yielding JEPI due to the 15% tax rate on qualified dividends versus the 22% ordinary income tax rate. Keep in mind that the tax rate on qualified dividends maxes out at 20% when you start making more than $500K, while ordinary income tax rates scale up to as much as 37% as your income rises. That said, please note that this is not tax advice, and it is always recommended to consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation. Tax laws and regulations vary from state to state, not to mention from country to country.

Buy GPIX and SPYI Instead of JEPI

While there once was a time when JEPI was the king of covered call funds, that is no longer the case today with the numerous funds that have launched since. If we only consider funds that sell monthly covered calls, GPIX and SPYI are much better alternatives than JEPI for most income investors.

First, their distributions are much more stable. GPIX targets an 8.5% distribution rate, while SPYI targets a 12% distribution rate. Comparatively, JEPI's yields range as high as 12.23% or as low as 6.59%. This, however, isn't considering that both SPYI and GPIX have the capability of offering more tax-efficient distributions through using tax-loss harvesting strategies to produce return of capital ("ROC") distributions that lower your cost-basis in the ETF and defer taxes until you sell or when your cost-basis reaches zero. SPYI also has the benefit of using 1256 index contracts that qualify for 60/40 long-term short-term capital gains tax rates. Thus, you are more likely to end up with more income in your pockets investing in GPIX or SPYI instead of JEPI, especially if you are factoring in taxes.

Of course, the risk is that GPIX and SPYI have not been around nearly as long as JEPI and are smaller than JEPI. GPIX was launched in October 2023 and has $482M in AUM while SPYI was launched in August 2022 and has over $3.02B in AUM. Both pale in comparison to JEPI, which was launched in May 2020 and has amassed over $39B in AUM. This limited history means we don't get to see how GPIX or SPYI performed during the 2022 bear market nor the 2020 pandemic crash (which we got to see how JEPI may have performed via JEPIX). Fortunately, GPIX and SPYI's strategies are much more transparent. Their call options are managed themselves, and the underlying holdings simply track the S&P 500. During down markets, we can expect the two funds to fall almost just as much as the S&P 500, with the only thing offsetting the losses being call option premiums.

The benefit, however, is that GPIX and SPYI are far better at capturing upside than JEPI. So even if the funds drop more than JEPI, we can expect the two funds to capture the recovery upside much better than JEPI, especially considering that GPIX and SPYI will have an easier time reaching their target distributions when volatility is high and can open up larger portions of their portfolio for capital appreciation. Keep in mind that JEPI is not guaranteed to reduce volatility significantly too. The pandemic crash showed how JEPI could drop as much as the S&P 500, and in cases where losses are not preventable, you'd rather position yourself for the recovery.

Essentially, GPIX and SPYI offer more upside than JEPI and more stable income that's often higher and more tax-efficient. When these two funds exist, not to mention other strong-performing S&P 500 covered call funds like various 0DTE funds, it has become difficult for me to justify buying JEPI. Yes, JEPI offers lower volatility and has proven itself to do so during 2022. This lower volatility is not guaranteed, however, as the fund can still drop just as much as the overall market as shown by how the mutual fund version, JEPIX, performed during the 2020 pandemic crash. Combine this with the volatility and tax inefficiency of JEPI's distributions, and it becomes difficult to continue justify holding JEPI. Thus, I rate JEPI a Sell and recommend income investors look at GPIX and SPYI as alternatives. I have written multiple pieces on the two funds linked below as initial points of research.

GPIX Vs. SPYI: How To Choose The Right Fund For You

GPIX: Capturing More Upside Than Other Monthly S&P 500 Buy-Write Funds

SPYI: Buy This For The 12% Target Distributions
